lines.en_lit: Joshua, for whom manna fell corresponding all the Jewish people, “Man” is written here, and “man” is written there: “Take to you Joshua, the son of Nun, a man in whom there is spirit, Say Moses, it is written: “Now the man Moses was very humble” We can learn “man” from the word “man,” but we cannot learn “man” from “the man,”

students of Rabbi Shimon ben Yoḥai asked him: Why didn’t the manna fall for the Jewish people once a year He said to them: I will give you a parable: To what does this matter compare? To a king of flesh and blood who has one son. He granted him food once a year and greeted his father only once a year, he arose and granted him his food every day, and visited him every day.

So too, the Jewish people, someone who had four or five children would be worried and say: Perhaps the manna will not fall tomorrow and we will all die of starvation. Consequently, everyone directed their hearts to their Father in heaven
Alternatively, so that they would eat it while it was hot Alternatively, due to carrying on the journey.

Rabbi Tarfon, and Rabbi Yishmael, and the Elders were sitting and discussing the passage about the manna and Rabbi Elazar HaModa’i was sitting among them. Rabbi Elazar HaModa’i responded and said: The manna that fell for the Jewish people was sixty cubits high. Rabbi Tarfon said to him: Moda’i, how long will you collect words and bring upon us

He said to him: Rabbi, I am interpreting a verse. “Fifteen cubits above did the waters prevail; and the mountains were covered” Is it possible that it would be fifteen cubits from a valley, fifteen from the plain, fifteen from the mountains? Did the water stand as though in layers, Furthermore, how could the Ark travel Rather: “On the same day were all the fountains of the great deep broken up” until the water was level with the mountains. Afterward, “fifteen cubits above did the waters prevail.”

But which attribute is greater, the attribute of goodness or the attribute of retribution? One must say the attribute of goodness than the attribute of retribution. With regard to the attribute of retribution, states: “And the windows of heaven were opened” with regard to the attribute of goodness, states: “He commanded the skies above, and opened the doors of heaven, and rained down manna upon them to eat and gave them heavenly grain”
how many windows are in a door? Four. four This equals eight it turns out that the manna that fell for the Jewish people was sixty cubits high,

it was taught Isi ben Yehuda said: The manna that fell for the Jewish people would accumulate and ascend until all the kings of the East and West could see it, as it is stated: “You prepared a table before me in the presence of my enemies; my cup runs over” “My cup runs over [revaya]”; Abaye said: We learn from this that the cup of David in the next world holds 221 log, since it is stated: “My cup runs over,” revaya has that numerical value.
This is not similar, There, forty days; here, one hour there for everyone here was for the Jewish people alone. And there much more for them Rabbi Elazar HaModa’i derived “opening” “opening”

prohibited in eating These five afflictions what correspond? Rav Ḥisda said: the five afflictions in the Torah. “And on the tenth “But on the tenth a Shabbat of solemn rest, “It is a Shabbat of solemn rest [shabbaton] “And it shall be for you

Are these five We learn six: Drinking is included in eating; As Reish Lakish said: From where do we derive that drinking is included in eating? As it is stated: “And you shall eat before the Lord your God, the tithe of your grain, your wine [tirosh], and your oil” Tirosh is wine, calls “and you shall eat,”
From where Perhaps was eaten as anigron, Rabba bar Shmuel said: Anigron beet juice Akhsigron water that comes from boiled vegetables

Rather, Rav Aḥa bar Ya’akov said: From here “And you shall spend that money on all that your soul desires, on oxen, or on sheep, or on wine, or on strong drink [sheikhar] Strong drink is a drink, but the Merciful One calls “and you shall eat.”

From where Perhaps here too it was eaten as anigron? writes “strong drink [sheikhar],” something intoxicates. Perhaps a sweet dried fig from Keilah. As it was taught ate a sweet dried fig from Keilah or drank honey or milk, and entered the Temple
